principles.label: Govern AI applications, not AI technology
principles.summary: Safety is a function of the application, not the underlying technology. Regulating base foundation models stifles open-source innovation and misses the actual risks, which depend entirely on how the AI is implemented downstream.
principles.details: Like an electric motor, AI is a general-purpose tool. It cannot be inherently safe or unsafe; it depends entirely on whether it is used responsibly in a specific application (e.g., a medical device vs. a chatbot).
principles.representative_quote: we should be focus on governing the applications because risks depend much more on the application not on the technology.

principles.label: Everyone should learn to code in the AI era
principles.summary: As AI makes coding easier and more accessible, the ability to steer a computer to do exactly what you want becomes a superpower for all roles, not just software engineers.
principles.details: Advising people not to learn to code because AI will automate it is terrible career advice. People who use AI to code will be vastly more productive and have more fun, leading to a societal shift where coding is a daily workflow for everyone from marketers to recruiters.
principles.representative_quote: Some senior business leaders were recently advising others to not learn to code on the grounds that AI will automate coding. We’ll look back on that as some of the worst career advice ever given.

principles.label: AI automates tasks, not jobs
principles.summary: Jobs are composed of many distinct tasks. AI is best understood and implemented by analyzing work at the task level to see where it can automate or augment, rather than viewing it as a wholesale replacement for human jobs.
principles.details: Automating 20-30% of a worker's tasks makes them more productive and increases the marginal value of their labor, freeing them up to focus on the rest of their job.
principles.representative_quote: from a business perspective I find it more useful to think of AI as timating tasks rather than jobs because jobs are made out of many tasks

principles.label: The application layer must generate the most value
principles.summary: For the underlying AI infrastructure (semiconductors, cloud, foundation models) to succeed economically, the applications built on top must generate enough revenue to pay for those lower layers.
principles.details: Because AI is a general-purpose technology, it has countless use cases across different industries. Identifying and building these specific, valuable applications is the most important work ahead.
principles.representative_quote: we need the applications to generate even even more Revenue so that they can afford to pay the technology layers underneath them

principles.label: Drive the cost of proof-of-concepts to zero
principles.summary: Because AI accelerates prototyping by 10x or more, teams should focus on building many cheap prototypes to find the few great ideas, rather than trying to force every prototype into production.
principles.details: Execution speed is the strongest predictor of a startup's success. The ability to build, validate, or falsify ideas quickly allows teams to iterate toward product-market fit before running out of resources.
principles.representative_quote: I would rather focus on driving the cost of those 18 proof of concepts towards zero. And this is becoming a new mechanism for teams to invent.

principles.label: Technology that unlocks creativity creates jobs
principles.summary: Historically, whenever technology unlocks human creativity, it expands opportunities and creates far more jobs than it destroys.
principles.details: The world is generally better off with more intelligence in it. AI-driven job growth has already started, following historical trends where creative technologies expand opportunities rather than just eliminating them.
principles.representative_quote: Whenever technology unlocks human creativity, it creates far more jobs than it destroys.

principles.label: In times of uncertainty, build on stable foundations
principles.summary: During periods of economic and technological flux, personal skills and professional communities are the resilient assets most likely to weather shifts in business and society.
principles.representative_quote: In times of economic and technological uncertainty, build on things that tend to remain stable. Community and skills are likely to weather shifts in business and society.

principles.label: AI-native software development requires generalists
principles.summary: As AI accelerates the coding process, software engineering teams operate differently, requiring teammates to take on a wider variety of roles.
principles.representative_quote: As AI accelerates software development, teammates must play a wider variey of roles AI-native software engineering teams operate very differently than traditional teams.

principles.label: Fast feedback loops are critical for iteration
principles.summary: Waiting months for traditional feedback (like academic peer review) stalls progress. Agentic AI workflows can provide rapid, actionable feedback to tighten iteration cycles.
principles.details: When tested on complex tasks like reviewing research papers, agentic AI is approaching human-level performance, making it a viable tool for accelerating feedback.
principles.representative_quote: Their feedback loop -- waiting ~6 months for feedback each time -- was painfully slow. We wanted to see if an agentic workflow can help researchers iterate faster.

principles.label: Scaling data and compute drives performance
principles.summary: Scaling up data and compute infrastructure leads to massive, predictable performance improvements in AI capabilities.
principles.details: This was the foundational proof point for the Google Brain team, demonstrating that leveraging massive compute infrastructure and data could transform AI capabilities and drive business applications.
principles.representative_quote: Using Google's compute infrastructure, we started by proving that scaling up data and compute leads to huge performance improvements

principles.label: Apply a data-centric approach to ML
principles.summary: Model performance can be significantly improved by focusing on tuning the data itself through synthesis or augmentation, rather than solely tuning the model architecture.
principles.representative_quote: Apply “data-centric AI” to not only tune your model but tune your data (using data synthesis or data augmentation) to improve your model’s performance.

frameworks.label: Agentic Workflow Iteration
frameworks.summary: A loop for generating high-quality, complex output from an LLM by mimicking human cognitive processes of research, drafting, and revision.
frameworks.details: Steps: 1) Prompt the AI to write an outline. 2) Have the AI perform web research to fetch context. 3) Generate a first draft. 4) Have the AI read, critique, and revise the draft. 5) Repeat the loop iteratively to improve the work product.
frameworks.representative_quote: we end up with this iterative workflow where your model does some thinking and some research does some revision goes back to do more thinking and by going around this loop many times.

frameworks.label: Task-Based Automation Analysis
frameworks.summary: A method for evaluating how AI will impact a specific job, business, or industry by breaking roles down into component tasks.
frameworks.details: Steps: 1) Look at what people are doing in a specific sector. 2) Break the jobs down into their component tasks. 3) Identify the subset of tasks that are amenable to AI automation. 4) Automate those specific tasks to free up workers to focus on the rest of their job and increase productivity.
frameworks.representative_quote: break jobs down into tasks, then usually there can often be a subset of tasks. There's some that are amenable to AI automation and that recipe is helping a lot of businesses create value

frameworks.label: Rapid Product Feedback Hierarchy
frameworks.summary: A tiered approach to quickly validating product decisions, moving from the fastest (but potentially less accurate) to the slowest methods.
frameworks.details: Steps: 1) Rely on your own gut instinct as a subject matter expert. 2) Ask 3 friends or teammates to play with the product. 3) Sit in a high-traffic area and respectfully ask 3-10 strangers for feedback. 4) Send prototypes to 100 testers. 5) Run an A/B test (the slowest method).

frameworks.label: Corporate AI Innovation Process
frameworks.summary: A systematic five-step process for large corporations to identify and implement AI opportunities.
frameworks.details: Steps: 1) Provide executive training on AI technology. 2) Brainstorm AI application opportunities using a task-based analysis. 3) Conduct diligence on project feasibility and value. 4) Prioritize projects and make build vs. buy vs. spin-out decisions. 5) Invest in long-term AI strategy and workforce training.

mental_models.label: The Electric Motor Analogy
mental_models.summary: A lens for understanding AI regulation and safety. An AI model is a general-purpose technology like an electric motor: it can be used in a dialysis machine (beneficial) or a smart bomb (harmful). You must regulate the application, not the motor itself.
mental_models.details: When policymakers try to regulate foundation models, it is like asking an electric motor maker to guarantee their motor will never be used in an unsafe application. It is impossible and stifles innovation.
mental_models.representative_quote: AI is neither safe nor unsafe. It is how you apply it that makes it safe or unsafe.

mental_models.label: The 'Star Trek' Interface
mental_models.summary: Using science fiction as a lens to understand the future of human-computer interaction, specifically predicting that voice will eventually replace keyboard typing as the primary interface.
mental_models.details: Voice-based AI is improving rapidly, and most people still underestimate how pervasive voice user interfaces will become.
mental_models.representative_quote: I think people underestimate how big voice AI will get. If you look at “Star Trek” movies, no one envisioned everyone typing on the keyboard, right?

mental_models.label: Linear Typing vs. Agentic Workflow
mental_models.summary: A metaphor for understanding why zero-shot prompting limits LLMs. Standard prompting is like forcing a human to write an essay from the first word to the last without ever using the backspace key.
mental_models.details: Agentic workflows solve this by allowing the AI to research, draft, review, and revise, mimicking human cognitive processes and drastically improving output quality.

mental_models.label: The AI Stack
mental_models.summary: A layered view of the AI industry: Semiconductors at the bottom, Cloud/Hyperscalers above that, Foundation Models next, and the Application Layer at the top.
mental_models.details: The Application Layer holds the biggest opportunities because it must generate the massive revenue required to sustain and pay for the lower infrastructure layers.
mental_models.representative_quote: it turns out the only way for the technology suppliers to be successful is if the application builders are even more successful.

mental_models.label: Two-Way Door Tech Stacks
mental_models.summary: Because AI coding assistants have drastically lowered the cost and time of software engineering, architectural decisions that used to be irreversible 'one-way doors' are increasingly becoming reversible 'two-way doors'.
mental_models.details: It is now feasible to throw away a codebase after a week and rebuild it from scratch on a new tech stack because it is cheap and fast to do so.

mental_models.label: Training vs. Inference Dichotomy
mental_models.summary: Evaluating the AI business landscape by separating the highly speculative, capital-intensive 'training' phase from the high-utility, high-demand 'inference' phase.
mental_models.details: While the massive capital expenses in the pretraining phase might face a bubble, the demand for querying preset AI systems (inference) is massive and guaranteed to grow.
mental_models.representative_quote: Inference demand is massive, and I’m very confident inference demand will continue to grow.

heuristics.label: Build on stable foundations in uncertain times
heuristics.summary: In times of economic and technological uncertainty, build on things that tend to remain stable, like community and skills.
heuristics.representative_quote: In times of economic and technological uncertainty, build on things that tend to remain stable. Community and skills are likely to weather shifts in business and society.

heuristics.label: Move fast and be responsible
heuristics.summary: An update to the old Silicon Valley mantra 'move fast and break things' for the AI era.
heuristics.details: The most responsible teams actually move very fast, but they mitigate risk by testing software in safe sandbox environments to find errors before broad release.
heuristics.representative_quote: I think the Mantra is move fast and be responsible.

heuristics.label: Build 20 prototypes, let 18 die
heuristics.summary: Build 20 prototypes to see what sticks, and let 18 die a quick quiet death.
heuristics.details: Focus on driving the cost of proof-of-concepts to zero rather than trying to get every prototype into production.
heuristics.representative_quote: I would rather focus on driving the cost of those 18 proof of concepts towards zero.

heuristics.label: Write insecure code for local prototypes
heuristics.summary: Go ahead and write insecure code for quick local prototypes, but secure it before shipping.
heuristics.details: Encourages rapid prototyping by lowering the requirements for early-stage, local proof-of-concepts.
heuristics.representative_quote: I routinely go to my team and say, 'Go ahead, write insecure code.' Because if this software is only going to run on your laptop and you don't plan to maliciously hack your own laptop, it's fine.

heuristics.label: Ignore token costs early on
heuristics.summary: To a first approximation, don't worry about how much tokens cost until you have enough users to make it a problem.
heuristics.details: Most startups won't get enough traction for token costs to matter. If it becomes a problem, it means you have users, and you can engineer solutions later.

heuristics.label: Scale cheap tasks exponentially
heuristics.summary: If you can make a task a thousand times cheaper, do this task a thousand times more.

heuristics.label: Use agents for slow feedback loops
heuristics.summary: Use agentic workflows to tighten painfully slow feedback loops.
heuristics.representative_quote: Their feedback loop -- waiting ~6 months for feedback each time -- was painfully slow. We wanted to see if an agentic workflow can help researchers iterate faster.

signature_quotes.label: AI is the new electricity
signature_quotes.summary: Explaining why AI will impact every industry.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: AI is the new electricity because I think AI like electricity is a general purpose technology meaning it has a lot of use cases

signature_quotes.label: Automating tasks, not jobs
signature_quotes.summary: Explaining how businesses should analyze AI automation opportunities.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: I think of AI as automating tasks rather than jobs.

signature_quotes.label: AI is neither safe nor unsafe
signature_quotes.summary: Discussing why he prefers the term 'Responsible AI' over 'AI Safety'.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: AI is neither safe nor unsafe. It is how you apply it that makes it safe or unsafe.

signature_quotes.label: Worst career advice ever given
signature_quotes.summary: Criticizing the advice that people shouldn't learn to code because AI will automate it.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: Some senior business leaders were recently advising others to not learn to code on the grounds that AI will automate coding. We’ll look back on that as some of the worst career advice ever given.

signature_quotes.label: Build on stable foundations
signature_quotes.summary: Advice to professionals feeling job insecurity due to AI.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: In times of economic and technological uncertainty, build on things that tend to remain stable. Community and skills are likely to weather shifts in business and society.

signature_quotes.label: Concrete vs Vague ideas
signature_quotes.summary: Explaining why startups must focus on concrete ideas to maintain execution speed.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: When you're vague, you're almost always right. But when you're concrete, you may be right or wrong. Either way is fine. We can discover that much more fast.

signature_quotes.label: AI is amazing and highly limited
signature_quotes.summary: Discussing the public's perception of AI capabilities.

signature_quotes.label: Technology unlocks creativity
signature_quotes.summary: Discussing the impact of AI on the job market.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: Whenever technology unlocks human creativity, it creates far more jobs than it destroys.

anti_patterns.label: Spreading AI doomerism and anti-AI propaganda
anti_patterns.summary: Running PR campaigns that tell the public not to trust AI or comparing it to nuclear weapons.
anti_patterns.details: These narratives are often PR tactics used to push for regulations that create moats and shut down open-source competition. They cause real-world harm by discouraging well-meaning people from entering the field and creating local resistance to necessary infrastructure.
anti_patterns.representative_quote: Right. As AI has taken off, we our community convinced people not to trust AI.

anti_patterns.label: Regulating base AI technology instead of applications
anti_patterns.summary: Implementing burdensome AI regulations that target foundation models and open-source software rather than specific use cases.
anti_patterns.details: It is impossible to guarantee a general-purpose technology will never be used dangerously. Attempting to do so leads to regulatory capture where only massive tech companies can afford compliance, destroying the open-source ecosystem.
anti_patterns.representative_quote: The biggest danger to that concentration is regulatory capture.

anti_patterns.label: Advising people not to learn to code
anti_patterns.summary: Telling professionals and students to avoid learning to code because AI will do it for them.
anti_patterns.details: As coding becomes easier, the ability to precisely steer a computer becomes more valuable. People who understand how to command computers will vastly outperform those who don't.
anti_patterns.representative_quote: I think we'll look back on this as some of the worst career advice ever given because as better tools make software engineing easier, more people should do it, not fewer.

anti_patterns.label: Analyzing AI's impact by looking at entire jobs
anti_patterns.summary: Believing that AI will wholesale replace entire professions (like radiologists or software engineers).
anti_patterns.details: Jobs are too broad. Breaking jobs down into specific tasks reveals the actual, actionable opportunities for AI automation and augmentation.

anti_patterns.label: Using LLMs exclusively in a linear workflow
anti_patterns.summary: Relying solely on zero-shot prompting without giving the AI a chance to iterate.
anti_patterns.details: It forces the AI to generate output in one continuous stream without the ability to research, reflect, or revise, which artificially limits the quality of its output.

anti_patterns.label: Pursuing vague, high-level ideas in startups
anti_patterns.summary: Focusing on broad concepts (e.g., 'use AI to optimize healthcare') rather than concrete implementations.
anti_patterns.details: Vague ideas get social validation but cannot be built quickly because different engineers will interpret them differently, destroying execution speed.

anti_patterns.label: Lamenting failed AI proof-of-concepts
anti_patterns.summary: Complaining that most AI proof-of-concepts don't make it into production.
anti_patterns.details: It misunderstands the new economics of AI development. The goal should be cheap experimentation, accepting that many prototypes will fail in order to find the few that scale.
